grammarLessons.name: 〜なければなりません — Must / Have To

cards.grammarNote:
Pain is expressed with **が痛い**: **頭が痛い** = my head hurts.
The body part takes **が** (subject particle) and **痛い** is an adjective — the body part becomes the subject of the pain.

**髪** (kami) = hair on the head. **毛** (ke) = body hair in general.
**髪が長い** (has long hair); **髪が短い** (has short hair).
cards.phraseRomanized: kanojo wa kami ga nagai desu

cards.grammarNote:
**顔** (kao) = face. Common compounds: **顔色** (kaoiro) = complexion/facial color, **笑顔** (egao) = smiling face.
**顔を洗う** (kao wo arau) = to wash one's face. The **を** marks **顔** as the direct object of **洗う** (to wash).
**〜てください** (te kudasai) = 'please do ~': a polite request form.

**目** (me) = eye. On-reading: **もく/ぼく** (moku/boku) as in **眼科** (ganka, ophthalmology).
**青い目をしている** (aoi me wo shite iru) = 'has blue eyes' — lit. 'is doing blue eyes'. **〜をしている** is a pattern for describing physical features: **丸い目をしている** (has round eyes).
**目が悪い** (me ga warui) = has poor eyesight.
cards.phrase: 彼は青い目をしています。
cards.phraseRomanized: kare wa aoi me wo shite imasu

cards.grammarNote:
**耳** (mimi) = ear. On-reading: **じ** (ji) as in **耳鼻科** (jibi-ka, ENT clinic — ear, nose).
**耳が痛い** (mimi ga itai) = my ear hurts. The body part is the subject with **が** and **痛い** (itai) is the adjective.
**耳が遠い** (mimi ga tōi) = hard of hearing; **耳がいい** (mimi ga ii) = has good hearing.

**鼻** (hana) = nose. Compounds: **鼻水** (hanamizu) = runny nose (lit. 'nose water'), **鼻血** (hanaji) = nosebleed.
**鼻が冷たい** (hana ga tsumetai) = my nose is cold. **冷たい** (tsumetai) = cold to the touch (for objects/body parts). Compare **寒い** (samui) = cold weather/air.

**口** (kuchi) = mouth. On-reading: **こう** (kō) as in **口腔** (kōkū, oral cavity).
**口を開ける** (kuchi wo akeru) = to open one's mouth. **開ける** (akeru) = to open (transitive); **開く** (aku) = to open (intransitive).
Body-part instructions use **〜てください**: **口を開けてください** = 'please open your mouth'.

**唇** (kuchibiru) = lip(s). The kanji combines **口** (kuchi, mouth) + **辱** — but in practice, the word is best learned as a unit.
**唇が赤い** (kuchibiru ga akai) = lips are red. The body-feature pattern **Xは Yが 形容詞** describes a physical feature: **彼女は唇が赤い** = 'Her lips are red'.
**赤い** (akai) = red — an い-adjective.
cards.phraseRomanized: kanojo wa kuchibiru ga akai desu

cards.grammarNote:
**歯** (ha) = tooth/teeth. On-reading: **し** (shi) in **歯科** (shika, dentistry).
**歯が痛い** (ha ga itai) = I have a toothache; lit. 'teeth hurt'. The standard pain pattern: body part + **が痛い**.
**歯を磨く** (ha wo migaku) = to brush one's teeth. **歯ブラシ** (haburashi) = toothbrush.

**舌** (shita) = tongue. Note that **下** (shita) also means 'below/under' — same pronunciation, different kanji.
**舌を出す** (shita wo dasu) = to stick out one's tongue; **出す** (dasu) = to put out/produce.
**舌打ち** (shitauchi) = clicking one's tongue (a sound of annoyance). **舌触り** (shitazawari) = texture in the mouth.

**首** (kubi) = neck. Also means 'head' in old or figurative usage: **首になる** = to be fired (lit. 'to become a head/neck' — as if decapitated).
**首が凝っている** (kubi ga kotte iru) = my neck is stiff. **凝る** (koru) = to stiffen/be tense — **〜ている** here describes an ongoing physical state.
Related: **肩が凝る** (kata ga koru) = stiff shoulders.

**腕** (ude) = arm. The **月** (flesh/meat radical, originally **肉**) on the left appears in many body part kanji: **腕** (arm), **胸** (chest), **腹** (belly), **腰** (waist).
**腕を骨折する** (ude wo kossetsu suru) = to fracture/break one's arm. **骨折** (kossetsu) = fracture (noun): **骨** (bone) + **折** (break).
**腕がいい** (ude ga ii) = to be skilled — a figurative use meaning 'good arm = good skills'.

**手** (te) = hand. On-reading: **しゅ** (shu) in **手術** (shujutsu, surgery).
**手を洗う** (te wo arau) = to wash one's hands. **洗う** (arau) = to wash.
**手** appears in many compound words: **上手** (jōzu, skillful), **下手** (heta, unskillful), **手伝う** (tetsudau, to help).

**指** (yubi) = finger or toe. **手の指** (te no yubi) = finger; **足の指** (ashi no yubi) = toe.
**指を切る** (yubi wo kiru) = to cut one's finger. **切る** (kiru) = to cut.
**指さす** (yubisasu) = to point at something. The prefix **指** + **さす** (to point) creates the compound verb.

**胸** (mune) = chest. The **月** (flesh radical) on the left shows this is a body part.
**胸が痛い** (mune ga itai) = chest hurts/heart aches. **胸が痛い** can describe both physical pain and emotional heartache.
**胸がドキドキする** (mune ga dokidoki suru) = heart is racing; **ドキドキ** = onomatopoeia for a pounding heart.
cards.phraseRomanized: kare wa mune ga itai desu

cards.grammarNote:
**背中** (senaka) = back (of the body). **背** (se/hai) alone = height/back; **中** (naka) = middle/inside.
**背中が痛い** (senaka ga itai) = my back hurts. Standard pain pattern: body part + **が痛い**.
**腰** (koshi) = lower back/waist; **背中** = upper/mid back. **腰痛** (yōtsū) = lower back pain.

**心臓** (shinzō) = the physical heart organ. **心** (kokoro) = the emotional/psychological heart/mind.
**心臓がドキドキする** (shinzō ga dokidoki suru) = heart is pounding; **ドキドキ** = onomatopoeia for a racing heartbeat.
**〜がしている** here means the heart is doing the action of pounding — the heart (subject with **が**) is performing **ドキドキする**.
cards.phrase: 心臓がドキドキしています。

cards.grammarNote:
**お腹** (onaka) = stomach/belly. The **お** prefix is an honorific that has become fixed with **腹** (hara/naka) — you almost always say **お腹** rather than just **腹** in polite speech.
**お腹が痛い** (onaka ga itai) = stomachache. **お腹が空く** (onaka ga suku) = to get hungry (lit. 'stomach empties').
Similarly, **お尻** (oshiri) = buttocks — always uses the **お** prefix in polite speech.

**筋肉** (kin'niku) = muscle. **筋** (kin/suji) = sinew/fiber; **肉** (niku) = flesh/meat.
The **月** radical in **肉** originally meant flesh — this radical appears in many body-part kanji.
**筋肉を痛める** (kin'niku wo itameru) = to hurt/strain a muscle. **痛める** (itameru) = to injure/hurt (transitive).
**筋肉痛** (kinnikutsū) = muscle soreness/DOMS.

**腰** (koshi) = waist/lower back. The **月** (flesh radical) shows it is a body part.
**腰が痛い** (koshi ga itai) = lower back hurts. **腰痛** (yōtsū) = chronic lower back pain.
**ウエスト** (uesuto) = waist measurement (loanword from English, used in the phrase here).
The body-feature pattern: **彼女はウエストが細い** = 'Her waist is slim'.
cards.phrase: 彼女はウエストが細いです。
cards.phraseRomanized: kanojo wa uesuto ga hosoi desu

cards.grammarNote:
**肌** (hada) = skin (especially skin as a surface, complexion). Compare **皮膚** (hifu) = skin (medical term).
**肌が柔らかい** (hada ga yawarakai) = skin is soft. **柔らかい** (yawarakai) = soft — an い-adjective.
Body feature pattern: **彼女は肌が柔らかい** = 'Her skin is soft'.
**肌荒れ** (hadaware) = rough/dry skin; **素肌** (suhada) = bare skin.
cards.phrase: 彼女は肌が柔らかいです。
cards.phraseRomanized: kanojo wa hada ga yawarakai desu

cards.grammarNote:
**血** (chi) = blood. On-reading: **けつ** (ketsu) in compounds: **血液** (ketsueki, blood — medical), **献血** (kenketsu, blood donation).
**献血する** (kenketsu suru) = to donate blood: **献** (ken) = to offer/dedicate + **血** (chi) = blood.
**血が出る** (chi ga deru) = to bleed (lit. 'blood comes out'); **血が止まる** (chi ga tomaru) = bleeding stops.

**脳** (nō) = brain. The **月** (flesh radical) appears here too. Compounds: **脳梗塞** (nōkōsoku, stroke), **脳波** (nōha, brain waves).
**複雑** (fukuzatsu) = complex/complicated — a な-adjective: **複雑な問題** (a complex problem).
**脳は複雑です** = 'The brain is complex' — **は** marks **脳** as the topic.

**頭蓋骨** (zugaikotsu) = skull: **頭蓋** (zugai, cranium) + **骨** (kotsu/hone, bone).
**骨** (hone) = bone; on-reading **こつ** (kotsu) in medical compounds: **骨折** (kossetsu, fracture), **骨盤** (kotsuban, pelvis).
**〜は〜を守る** (wa wo mamoru) = '~ protects ~': **頭蓋骨は脳を守る** = 'The skull protects the brain'. **守る** (mamoru) = to protect/guard.
cards.phrase: 頭蓋骨は脳を守ります。

cards.grammarNote:
**胸** (mune) = chest/breast — the same kanji is used for both chest (card: chest) and breast depending on context.
**〜に痛みを感じる** (ni itami wo kanjiru) = 'to feel pain in ~': the location takes **に**, and **痛み** (pain, noun) takes **を** as the direct object of **感じる** (to feel).
Compare: **胸が痛い** (chest hurts, adjective pattern) vs **胸に痛みを感じる** (feel pain in the chest, noun pattern).
cards.englishPhrase: She felt pain in her chest.
cards.phrase: 彼女は胸に痛みを感じました。
cards.phraseRomanized: kanojo wa mune ni itami wo kanjimashita

cards.grammarNote:
**足** (ashi) = leg or foot — Japanese uses the same word for both. Context or a modifier clarifies: **足の裏** (sole of foot), **足の指** (toe).
**足が疲れている** (ashi ga tsukarete iru) = legs are tired. **疲れる** (tsukareru) = to get tired; in **〜ている** form it describes the resulting state of tiredness.

**足** (ashi) can mean both 'leg' and 'foot' depending on context.
**足の裏** = the sole of the foot; **足の指** = the toe.

**足の指** (ashi no yubi) = toe; lit. 'finger of the foot'. The **の** links the possessor (foot) to the possessed (finger/toe).
**ぶつける** (butsukeru) = to knock/bang into something — a common mishap verb.
**足の指をぶつける** = to stub a toe. The **を** marks the toe as the object of the action.
cards.phrase: 彼は足の指をぶつけました。
cards.phraseRomanized: kare wa ashi no yubi wo butsukemashita

cards.grammarNote:
**関節** (kansetsu) = joint: **関** (kan) = connection + **節** (setsu) = segment/node.
**〜くなっている** (ku natte iru) = has become more ~ (change of state that persists): **固くなっている** = has become stiff and remains so.
**固い** (katai) = hard/stiff — an い-adjective. **固く** is the adverbial form: modifies the verb **なる**.
cards.phrase: 関節が固くなっています。

cards.grammarNote:
**息** (iki) = breath. Compounds: **息子** (musuko, son — lit. 'breath-child'), **呼吸** (kokyū, breathing/respiration).
**息を止める** (iki wo tomeru) = to hold one's breath; lit. 'to stop the breath'. **止める** (tomeru) = to stop (transitive).
**息をする** (iki wo suru) = to breathe; **深呼吸する** (shinkokyū suru) = to take a deep breath.

**見る** (miru) = to look at/watch (intentional action).
**見える** (mieru) = to be visible / can see (potential/spontaneous): **はっきり見える** = can see clearly.
The phrase uses **見えます**, not **見ます** — this is a key distinction: **見る** is volitional (I look), **見える** is non-volitional (it is visible, I can see).

**聞く** (kiku) = to listen/ask (intentional).
**聞こえる** (kikoeru) = to be audible / can hear (spontaneous/potential): **聞こえません** = 'cannot hear / inaudible'.
Just like **見る/見える**, Japanese distinguishes: **聞く** (I listen) vs **聞こえる** (it is heard/audible). The phrase uses **聞こえません** — the sound doesn't reach me.

**嗅ぐ** (kagu) = to sniff/smell (intentional). **匂い** (nioi) = smell/scent (noun).
**匂いがする** (nioi ga suru) = 'a smell is happening / there is a smell' — the smell is the subject with **が**, and **する** conveys a sensory impression.
This **する** pattern for senses: **音がする** (a sound is heard), **味がする** (a taste is detected).

**味わう** (ajiwau) = to savor/taste (intentional, often implies appreciation).
**味** (aji) = taste/flavor (noun). **味見する** (ajimi suru) = to taste a sample — used in the example phrase.
**味がする** = 'it has a taste' (sensory). **美味しい** (oishii) = delicious — a very common い-adjective.
cards.phrase: このスープを味見してください。
cards.phraseRomanized: kono sūpu wo ajimi shite kudasai

cards.grammarNote:
**触る** (sawaru) = to touch/feel (intransitive; takes **に** for what is touched).
**触れる** (fureru) = to touch/come into contact with (slightly more formal/gentle).
**触らないでください** = 'please don't touch': **〜ないでください** (naide kudasai) = polite prohibition.
Note: **触らないで！** (without **ください**) = 'Don't touch!' — more urgent/casual.

**呼吸する** (kokyū suru) = to breathe/respire. **呼吸** (kokyū) = breathing/respiration (noun).
**ゆっくり** (yukkuri) = slowly/leisurely — an adverb placed before the verb.
**深呼吸する** (shinkokyū suru) = to take a deep breath: **深** (shin/fuka, deep) + **呼吸**.
**息をする** (iki wo suru) is a more casual alternative for 'to breathe'.
cards.phrase: ゆっくり呼吸してください。

cards.grammarNote:
**曲げる** (mageru) = to bend (transitive). **曲がる** (magaru) = to bend/turn (intransitive).
**膝を曲げる** (hiza wo mageru) = to bend one's knees. **膝** (hiza) = knee.
**膝** is a common body-part instruction word in physical therapy and exercise contexts. **膝を曲げてください** = 'Please bend your knees'.

**伸ばす** (nobasu) = to stretch/extend (transitive). **伸びる** (nobiru) = to stretch/grow (intransitive).
**腕を伸ばす** (ude wo nobasu) = to stretch/extend one's arms. **腕** (ude) = arm.
Related: **背を伸ばす** (se wo nobasu) = to straighten one's posture; **髪を伸ばす** (kami wo nobasu) = to grow out one's hair.
cards.phrase: 腕を伸ばしてください。

cards.grammarNote:
**肩** (kata) = shoulder. The **月** (flesh radical) is present here.
**肩が痛い** (kata ga itai) = my shoulder hurts. Standard pain pattern with **が** subject + **痛い**.
**肩が凝る** (kata ga koru) = stiff/tight shoulders — very common in Japan; **肩こり** (katakori) = shoulder stiffness (a common complaint).

**膝** (hiza) = knee.
**けがする** (kega suru) = to get injured; **けが** (kega) = injury/wound.
**膝をけがする** (hiza wo kega suru) = to injure one's knee. **を** marks the knee as the object.
**膝が笑う** (hiza ga warau) = 'knees are laughing' — idiom for wobbly legs from fatigue. A colorful way Japanese describes tired, shaking knees.

**肘** (hiji) = elbow. The **月** (flesh radical) is visible in the kanji.
**肘をつく** (hiji wo tsuku) = to lean on one's elbow / prop oneself up on an elbow. **つく** here means to prop/rest against.
**肘をつかないで** = 'don't lean on your elbow' — **〜ないで** (without **ください**) is a softer, more casual prohibition.

**筋肉を緊張させる** (kin'niku wo kinchō saseru) = to flex/tense the muscles. **緊張** (kinchō) = tension (also used for nervousness).
The example phrase **筋肉に力を入れる** (kin'niku ni chikara wo ireru) = 'put strength into the muscles' — a more natural everyday way to say 'flex'.
**力を入れる** (chikara wo ireru) = to apply force/strength: **力** (chikara) = strength/power; **入れる** (ireru) = to put in.
cards.phrase: 筋肉に力を入れてください。
cards.phraseRomanized: kin'niku ni chikara wo irete kudasai

cards.grammarNote:
**まばたき** (mabataki) = a blink (noun). **まばたきする** = to blink.
**まばたきしないでください** = 'please don't blink': **〜しないでください** = please don't do ~.
**目をぱちぱちさせる** (me wo pachipachi saseru) = to blink repeatedly — **ぱちぱち** is the onomatopoeia for blinking.
cards.phrase: まばたきしないでください。

cards.grammarNote:
**汗をかく** (ase wo kaku) = to sweat/perspire; lit. 'to scratch sweat'. **汗** (ase) = sweat; **かく** here is an idiomatic verb meaning to produce/secrete.
**走ると汗をかく** = 'when I run, I sweat'. **〜と** as conditional: 'when/if you do X, Y naturally follows'.
Other **かく** body expressions: **くしゃみをかく** (to sneeze — less common form).

**出血する** (shukketsu suru) = to bleed (medical/formal): **出** (de, exit) + **血** (chi, blood).
The everyday expression: **血が出る** (chi ga deru) = 'blood comes out / is bleeding'.
**傷口から血が出ている** = 'blood is coming out from the wound': **傷口** (kizuguchi) = wound opening; **から** = from; **〜ている** describes ongoing state.
cards.phrase: 傷口から血が出ています。
cards.phraseRomanized: kizuguchi kara chi ga dete imasu

cards.grammarNote:
**感じる** (kanjiru) = to feel/sense (physically or emotionally).
In the example, **気分** (kibun) = mood/how one feels (noun). **気分はどうですか** = 'How do you feel?' — a standard inquiry about someone's wellbeing.
**どう** (dō) = how/in what way. **気分がいい** = feel good; **気分が悪い** = feel bad/nauseous.

**考える** (kangaeru) = to think/consider (deliberate, analytical thinking).
**思う** (omou) = to feel/think (intuitive, emotional thoughts): **〜と思う** = 'I think that ~'.
**何を考えていますか** = 'What are you thinking about?': **〜ている** with a mental verb = currently engaged in thinking.
**何** (nani/nan) = what.

**動く** (ugoku) = to move (intransitive). **動かす** (ugokasu) = to move something (transitive).
**動かせない** (ugokasena i) = cannot move (something): the potential negative of **動かす**.
**腕が動かせません** = 'I cannot move my arm' — the arm (**腕が**) is the subject of the inability. Note how the body part becomes the grammatical subject.

**骨っぽい** (honeppoi) = bony. **〜っぽい** is a suffix meaning '-ish / having the quality of': **骨** (bone) + **っぽい** = bone-ish/bony.
Other examples: **子供っぽい** (childish), **男っぽい** (masculine/manly).
**骨ばっている** (honebatte iru) = is bony/prominent-boned — the form used in the example: **骨張る** (honebaru) = to be bony/angular.
cards.phrase: 彼の手は骨ばっています。
cards.phraseRomanized: kare no te wa honebatte imasu

cards.grammarNote:
**毛深い** (kefukai) = hairy: **毛** (ke) = body hair + **深い** (fukai) = deep/thick.
The example uses **腕の毛が濃い** (ude no ke ga koi) = 'the arm hair is thick/dark'. **濃い** (koi) = thick/dense/dark (used for hair density, colors, flavors).
**毛** (ke) = body/general hair; **髪** (kami) = head hair. These are distinct in Japanese.
cards.phrase: 彼は腕の毛が濃いです。
cards.phraseRomanized: kare wa ude no ke ga koi desu

cards.grammarNote:
**柔らかい** (yawarakai) = soft/gentle/tender — an い-adjective.
**柔らかい** describes texture: **柔らかいパン** (soft bread), **柔らかい筋肉** (supple muscles).
Opposite: **硬い** (katai) = hard/stiff. The て-form **柔らかくて** connects to another adjective: **柔らかくて温かい** (soft and warm).
cards.phrase: ティッシュは柔らかいです。

cards.grammarNote:
**痛い** (itai) = hurts/sore/painful — an い-adjective.
**筋肉痛** (kinnikutsū) = muscle soreness (DOMS): a compound noun, not an adjective. **筋肉痛です** = 'I have muscle soreness'.
**痛い** is versatile: **頭が痛い** (headache), **お腹が痛い** (stomachache). Simply state body part + **が痛い**.

**左** (hidari) = left. **右** (migi) = right. These direction words precede the body part directly without a particle: **左膝** (hidari hiza) = left knee.
**左膝を痛める** (hidari hiza wo itameru) = to injure one's left knee. **痛める** (itameru) = to hurt/damage (transitive).
**痛める** vs **痛い**: **痛める** = to actively hurt something; **痛い** = it hurts (adjective state).

**右** (migi) = right. Placed directly before a body part: **右腕** (migiude) = right arm.
**骨折する** (kossetsu suru) = to fracture/break a bone: **骨** (kotsu/hone, bone) + **折** (setsu/o, break).
**右腕を骨折する** = to break one's right arm. **を** marks the arm as the object of the fracture.

**上半身** (jōhanshin) = upper body: **上** (jō, upper) + **半身** (hanshin, half-body).
**下半身** (kahanshin) = lower body. **半** (han) = half; **身** (shin) = body.
**上半身が強い** = 'the upper body is strong'. **強い** (tsuyoi) = strong — an い-adjective.

**下半身** (kahanshin) = lower body: **下** (ka, lower) + **半身** (hanshin, half-body).
**腰痛** (yōtsū) = lower back pain: **腰** (koshi, lower back/waist) + **痛** (tsū, pain).
**腰痛がある** (yōtsū ga aru) = 'there is lower back pain / to have lower back pain'. **がある** is used for conditions, symptoms, and states that exist.

**体** (karada) = body (everyday usage). **身体** (shintai) = body (more formal/medical term).
**人間の体** (ningen no karada) = the human body. **人間** (ningen) = human being.
**素晴らしい** (subarashii) = amazing/wonderful — an い-adjective.
**体の調子** (karada no chōshi) = physical condition: **調子** (chōshi) = condition/state.
cards.phrase: 人間の体は素晴らしいです。
cards.phraseRomanized: ningen no karada wa subarashii desu

cards.grammarNote:
**人間の** (ningen no) = human's / of humans — **の** makes **人間** a possessive/attributive modifier.
**人間** (ningen) = human being: **人** (hito/nin, person) + **間** (ma/kan, interval/between) — 'beings who exist between [heaven and earth]'.
**生物学** (seibutsugaku) = biology: **生** (life) + **物** (thing) + **学** (study). **〜学** (gaku) is the suffix for academic fields.
cards.phrase: 人間の生物学の一部です。
cards.phraseRomanized: ningen no seibutsugaku no ichibu desu
